EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system brought strong, damaging winds to northwest Oregon. The first round of strong winds arrived late Monday night/early Tuesday morning. The winds relaxed a bit as the frontal zone pushed into the region, then the front lifted back to the north with another round of strong south winds resulting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at the Yaquina Bridge with a gust to 62 kt. Strong winds were also reported at the Newport AWOS with a gust to to 52 kt, at Lincoln City with a gust to 66 kt, at Sea Lion Caves with a gust to 70 kt, and at the Newport C-MAN station with a gust to 53 kt. Highway 299 was closed due to downed trees and a semi-truck was blown over on US 101 at Waldport. Lincoln City reported a power outage for 2400 people.

Cape Blanco recorded gusts exceeding 57 mph intermittently from 27/1910 PST until 28/1740 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system brought strong, damaging winds to northwest Oregon. The first round of strong winds arrived late Monday night/early Tuesday morning. The winds relaxed a bit as the frontal zone pushed into the region, then the front lifted back to the north with another round of strong south winds resulting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at the Rockhouse RAWS  site with a gust to 64 kt. Highway 18 at Grande Ronde and Highway 126 at Tiernan Road were closed due to downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt resulting in a flood event on the Malheur River on the 17th and 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads from Harper and Westfall to Vale and Ontario were flooded when the Malheur River rose above flood stage and crested at 12.5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly clear skies and a cold air mass allowed temperatures to fall to near or below freezing this morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front stalled off the Oregon coast...leading to an extended high wind event for the coast and parts of interior Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous gusts exceeding High Wind Warning criteria were recorded at the Summer Lake RAWS during this interval.  The highest gust was 84 mph at 23/1138 PST.  A spotter 1N Summer Lake reported a gust to 66 mph at 23/1018 PST, and he reported that a semi-trailer was blown over by the strong gusts at milepost 89 of Oregon Highway 31.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded wind gusts exceeding 57 mph from 16/1238 PST until 17/0638 PST except for one observation.  The peak gust was 75 mph, recorded at 16/1438 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances over the weekend brought sub-tropical moisture to the Pacific Northwest producing heavy rainfall over the region. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff led to flooding on several rivers and streams in Northwest Oregon as well as landslides and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt runoff produced flooding along the Siletz River. The Siletz River at Siletz crested at 20.6 feet on January 16th at 17:30 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances over the weekend brought sub-tropical moisture to the Pacific Northwest producing heavy rainfall over the region. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff led to flooding on several rivers and streams in Northwest Oregon as well as landslides and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell over the Coast Range combined with snowmelt runoff to produce flooding along the Wilson River. Highway 101 in downtown Tillamook was flooded. The Wilson River at Tillamook crested at 13.9 feet on January 16th at 15:30 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific weather system brought strong southerly winds to the north and central Oregon Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Clatsop Spit with sustained winds of 36 kts and at Astoria NOS with gusts to 53 kts.

Strong gusts were also recorded by a spotter in Tillamook with 61 kts, the Astoria ASOS with 57 kts and a HAM operator in Astoria with 63 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable but strong west to northwest flow aloft produced scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF0 tornado, which was spotted by a resident, caused damage to a pump house on the farm south southwest of Lexington.  Insulation was seen twisting up in the air and 2x4s from the pump house landed 100 yards to the southeast.  Pea sized hail was seen prior to the damage and thunder was heard 10 minutes prior to the damage.  Trees, grass, and weeds were seen blowing in various directions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved over the Pacific Northwest. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the low triggered widespread thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and isolated large hail. This combined with the abundant spring snow-pack and wet ground to cause flooding and flash flooding.  As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Milkshakes Snotel (9) and High Ridge Snotel (7).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was accompanied by heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A catastrophic earthquake measuring 9.0 on the Richter scale occurred just off the coast of Japan on 03/11/2011.  This earthquake generated a Pacific-wide tsunami.  This tsunami struck the US west coast late in the morning on 03/11/2011.  No loss of life occurred in Oregon, but several ports and the boats in them were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first of a series of surges caused by the tsunami struck Port Orford at 07:11 PDT.  The maximum recorded amplitude was 2.02 meters which occurred around the seventh surge.  On the US West Coast, the only station that recorded a higher surge was Crescent City CA with a 2.47m amplitude. The surges gradually subsided during the next few days.  Major damage occurred at the Brookings Harbor, where 40% of the floating docks were destroyed and many boats were damaged or destroyed.  Docks, pilings, and channel buoys were also damaged at Gold Beach OR.  Four men were pulled into the ocean at Pistol River.  Two were rescued while the other two made it back to shore on their own.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A catastrophic earthquake measuring 9.0 on the Richter scale occurred just off the coast of Japan on 03/11/2011.  This earthquake generated a Pacific-wide tsunami.  This tsunami struck the US west coast late in the morning on 03/11/2011.  No loss of life occurred in Oregon, but several ports and the boats in them were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first of a series of surges caused by the tsunami struck Charleston Harbor at 07:15 PDT.  The maximum recorded amplitude was 72 centimeters which occurred on the fifth and ninth surges. The surges gradually subsided during the next few days.  Charleston Harbor sustained minor damage, but at least six boats broke loose from their moorings.  Minor damage was reported to a boat and dock in Winchester Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lasting winter storm moved through southern Oregon during this interval. The snow level started off above pass level with heavy rain totals reported at lower elevations. The snow level lowered to populated areas later in the event.  This wound up being a historic snowstorm for the Illinois Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Cave Junction reported 3.1 inches of snow in 24 hour ending at 16/1800 PST. then another 7.3 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 17/1800 PST.  A CoCoRAHs observer 2SW OBrien reported 11.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 17/1000 PST.  A CoCoRAHs observer 3.7E Cave Junction reported 11.2 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 17/1000 PST. A spotter 2NNE Selma reported 4.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 16/0800 PST and another 11.0 inches in 24 hours ending at 17/0800 PST.  A spotter 6SW Merlin reported 4.0 inches of snow in 8 hours ending at 17/0500 PST and another 4.0 inches in 10.5 hours ending at 17/0830 PST and another 1.5 inches in an hour ending at 17/0930 PST.  A spotter 2WSW OBrien reported 5.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 16/1030 PST, then another 7.0 inches in 20 hours ending at 17/0600 PST, then a storm total of 18.0 inches at 17/1800 PST.  He stated that this was the biggest snow in that area in 18 years.  He has been recorded weather data there for 20 years. A spotter 1SSW OBrien reported 12.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 17/0800 PST, 9.0 inches of it since 16/1800 PST. A spotter 5NNE Selma reported 11.0 inches of snow ending at 17/0800 PST. A spotter 2SE Cave Junction reported 9.5 inches of snow overnight ending at 17/0830 PST. A spotter 4SSW Merlin reported 7.8 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 17/0830 PST. A spotter 3ESE Grants Pass reported 5.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 17/0930 PST. A spotter 1WSW Williams reported 5.0 inches of snow in 12 hours ending at 17/0930 PST. A spotter 4W Merlin reported 9.0 inches of snow overnight ending at 17/0930 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold easterly wind through the Columbia River Gorge was keeping cold air trapped in the Gorge as a strong Pacific frontal system moved inland. This system spread precipitation over the Gorge and Upper Hood River Valley starting as snow and changing over to freezing rain as the air mass warmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Hood River reported 4.5 inches of new snow accumulation. Interstate 84 was closed due to ice and snow east of Troutdale around 04:00 PST on January 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall line developed over the coastal waters this day and moved onshore. Squall lines are unusual enough over the Oregon waters, but this one moved over the coastal range and brought severe winds and hail to the west side interior valleys...which is very unusual.  Many trees were uprooted or broken, and a number of major roads were closed by fallen trees.  75,000 homes lost power due to this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at Cape Arago recorded a gust to 86 mph at 13/1200 PST as the squall line moved through.  A spotter 2N Coos Bay reported a wind gust to 71 mph at 13/1417 PST as the squall line moved through.  The Signal Tree RAWS on the Coos/Douglas county boundary reported a gust to 61 mph at 13/1335 PST as the front moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances over the weekend brought sub-tropical moisture to the Pacific Northwest producing heavy rainfall over the region. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff led to flooding on several rivers and streams in Northwest Oregon as well as landslides and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With heavy rain and high tides hitting the North Coast Saturday night, Oregon Department of Transportation officials closed Highway 101 two miles south of Seaside from 08:20 PST. The Necanicum River and Beerman Creek overflowed their banks which caused about 15 inches of water on highway 101.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Campers at Morgan Lake reported quarter sized hail that destroyed tents and then afterward, winds gusting to 70 mph blew them over.  Hail completely covered the ground. One of the campers received minor injuries from the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved over the Pacific Northwest. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the low triggered widespread thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and isolated large hail. This combined with the abundant spring snow-pack and wet ground to cause flooding and flash flooding.  As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Milkshakes Snotel (9) and High Ridge Snotel (7).

A rapidly developing area of low pressure and its associated frontal system moved onto the central oregon coast and tracked northeast through the forecast area bringing strong gusty winds to the central Oregon Coast and portions of the western Oregon interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported by a trained spotter in Blodgett, Oregon with gusts to 55 kts.  Similar estimated reports were received in rural Lane County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low lying areas of Canyon City, John Day, Prairie City, and Mount Vernon were flooded along with other areas along the John Day River to the Picture Gorge area. The basement of the high school in Canyon City was flooded as were several businesses.

As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Milkshakes Snotel (9) and High Ridge Snotel (7).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Fork of the John Day River at Monument crested at 15.8 feet which was 1.8 feet above flood stage.  Flooding was also observed in Kimberly.

She and three other children were playing on a floatation toy in the surf about 30 feet from the shore. A wave swept the toy out from underneath them. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dollar Lake Fire was started by lightning on Friday August 26th, 2011. The fire has burned 6304 acres so far in the Mount Hood National Forest. The fire is 90% contained as of September 30th, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating and an unstable southwest flow aloft brought thunderstorms to mainly central Oregon which started the High Cascades Complex Fire and the Hancock Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked fires in the the Hancock Complex which had burned over 50,000 acres by the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving along a stalled cold front brought heavy snowfall to higher elevations of Grant County.  Snowfall amounts in inches included 7 miles east southeast of John Day (6) and Blue Mountain Spring SnoTel (5).  A car accident on slushy Highway 395 between Mount Vernon and Fox claimed two lives.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight south-north pressure gradient at the surface combined with strong winds aloft produced high wind gusts across north central Oregon. Peak wind gusts reported in mph included North Pole Ridge RAWS (61), Mountain Identifier (74), and Patjens RAWS (69).  Several downed trees caused multiple power outages in Bend and Sisters and prompted the closure of Shevlin Park.

A strong south-north pressure gradient produced damaging winds and blowing dust in Union and Umatilla counties. Peak winds in mph included LaGrande Airport (60), Union (58), and Ladd Canyon Rest Area (64).  A tree was knocked down in Pendleton.  Large tree branches were broken in Union, Cove and Milton-Freewater.  Visibility was reduced to around 1/4 mile in the Imbler area.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area sagged southeast from the Gulf of Alaska and brought cold air and heavy snowfall.  Snow amounts in inches included 2 miles south southwest of Camp Sherman (6), 5 miles north northwest of LaPine (6), Camp Sherman (6), and Sisters (6).  The snow caused numerous traffic accidents.
An early season cold air mass brought freezing temperatures to parts of south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 25 to 39 degrees.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cooler air mass and clearing skies made for cold low temperatures this night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 26 to 34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved over the Pacific Northwest. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the low triggered widespread thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and isolated large hail. This combined with the abundant spring snow-pack and wet ground to cause flooding and flash flooding.  As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Milkshakes Snotel (9) and High Ridge Snotel (7).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of rainfall within 1 hour caused flash flooding in the Heppner and Lexington areas.  Roads were inundated. A home, two basements, and a garage were flooded.  Sandbags were used to protect homes and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent showers with heavy rainfall of 1 to 2 inches produced flooding on Willow and Hinton Creeks.  Snowfall amounts in inches include 15 miles southeast of Heppner (6.5), Arbuckle Snotel (10), Madison Butte Snotel (6).
An unstable atmosphere ahead of an upper level trough produced severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 1.2 inches fell in less than 1 hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable atmosphere ahead of an upper level trough produced severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening.

This combined with the abundant spring snow-pack and wet ground to cause flooding and flash flooding.  As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Milkshakes Snotel (9) and High Ridge Snotel (7).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on above average snow pack produced record flood stage of 7.7 feet on the Grande Ronde River at Perry.  Additional flooding occurred on Little Creek, Wolf Creek, Ladd Creek, and Catherine Creek. Flooding impacted 12 thousand acres of crops and dozens of homes and businesses.  Twenty-county roads and state roads were covered including Highway 204 west of LaGrande.  Flooding also occurred at Hilgard and Red Bridge State Parks west of LaGrande.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough combined with daytime heating produced scattered thunderstorms over Union and Wallowa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved northeast through LaGrande toppling trees, snapping limbs, and downing power lines.  Hail and heavy rainfall with the storm dropped visibility to near zero, causing minor street flooding and a bicycle-vehicle accident which injured bicycle rider.  A baseball game in the Babe Ruth State Tournament was stopped for two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies, light winds and intense midday solar heating allowed a dust devil to develop near Hosmer Lake, west of Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dust devil moved over the lake and turned over 2 boats with passengers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Baker City experienced more minor flooding as the Powder River came out of its banks from the 20th through the 22nd of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River gauge observations and reports from local officials indicated that minor flooding was occuring in and around Baker City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought thunderstorms with gusty winds and locally heavy rainfall during the afternoon and overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported 1 inch of rain fell in 15 minutes.
